name: FaceTimeOS
oneLiner: Lets you call your own Mac on FaceTime and ask it to do things out loud. The computer actually does them for you and talks back to you during the call.
description: Have you ever gone to the gym and forgotten to train your Hugging Face model? Have you ever wanted to show a friend your most recent Fortnite clip, but the file was stuck on your Mac? We've all been there. In today's remote-first world, we're often physically separated from our most powerful tool: our personal computer. We're stuck on the go, desperately needing a local file, a specific app, or the ability to run a complex script that only exists on our Mac. Current remote desktop solutions are clunky, slow, and built for visual control, not quick, conversational commands. We were inspired to bridge this gap.
whyWon: You call your own Mac on FaceTime and tell it what to do out loud. It does the task while you're on the call and talks back to you like a person would. No other tool lets you run your computer through a normal video call.

name: Shepherd
oneLiner: A white cane for blind people with a small motor inside. Instead of just warning about obstacles, it gently pulls the user toward the safest open path, the way a guide dog would, and it costs about 20 times less than products in stores.
description: 1.7 million Americans are legally blind. While canes tell you something is there, they don't tell you where to go. Smart canes actively steer you around obstacles, but they’re prohibitively expensive and inaccessible. We wanted to create a cheaper, intelligent alternative. Shepherd is a motorized smart cane that uses iPhone LiDAR and computer vision to detect obstacles and physically guide the user away from them in real time, and is also able to navigate users using GPS with an interactive voice interface. A small motor on the cane applies lateral force, nudging the cane left or right, so the user feels which direction is clear without needing audio cues or screen interaction.
whyWon: Shepherd is a white cane with a motor in the handle that pushes your hand toward the open path. A blind person just follows it, the way they'd follow a guide dog. Other smart canes only warn you that something is in the way; this one actually steers you around it, and it costs about a twentieth as much.

name: Identity Matrix
oneLiner: An online game world where your character stays alive after you log off. It learns how you talk and behave, so your friends can still chat and hang out with a version of you while you're away.
description: What if your “online presence” didn’t stop when you closed your laptop? Identity Matrix is a real-time multiplayer simulation where your avatar transforms into a stateful agent. When you log off, your AI keeps running: it remembers how you talk, what you care about, who you avoid, and who you keep coming back to. Step 1: upload your identity to the Matrix. Step 2: find out you... We developed a real-time multiplayer virtual world with four core features: Our proudest feature is the Continual Learning loop we developed. The model iteratively learns from your mannerisms and interactions, storing this into our custom memory bank , optimized to remember both important things and minor details.
whyWon: Identity Matrix is an online world where your character keeps running after you log off. It learns how you talk and act, so your friends can keep chatting with a version of you while you're gone. In every other online game you just disappear when you leave; here you don't.

name: DIAL(*)
oneLiner: The team arrived at the hackathon with nowhere to sleep, so they built phone bots that called more than 100 real hotels overnight and negotiated a luxury room down to $158.
description: We were stranded in San Francisco on Saturday night without housing, and decided to let a fleet of AI agents find us a room. What started as a quick experiment turned into a full-stack system for autonomous negotiation, data-driven decision making, and natural communication. DIAL (*) is an optimized customer service agent designed for data-heavy use cases. It makes phone calls, speaks naturally, queries live databases, and learns from every conversation to improve future performance. Building a robust state machine that avoided hallucinations while staying conversational was hard. Integrating ElevenLabs’ voice with live Twilio calls also required solving real-time latency issues.
whyWon: The team had nowhere to sleep, so they built AI bots that called real hotels over the phone. The bots talked like people, haggled, and booked them a room for $158 that they actually slept in that night. It ran on live phone calls with real money, not a staged demo.

name: ROSS
oneLiner: A robot that helps blind people experience paintings. It draws the artwork on the palm of your hand, using warm and cold touches so you can feel where the different colors are.
description: Visual art is such a large component of human culture. If you visit any art gallery, you'll notice that many of the exhibits are paintings on 2D surfaces. What if you're blind and looking to experience the art as well? What if you could take a picture of a painting and feel the shapes and curves being drawn on your hands? Our inspiration for this project was based in accessibility, to make the idea of art accessible to the visually impaired by adding the elements of touch, sound, and temperature. The inspiration for the name specifically is Bob Ross, who believed that art should be accessible and joyful for everyone, regardless of skill or background.
whyWon: ROSS is a robot that draws a painting onto your palm so a blind person can feel it. It uses warm and cold touches to stand for different colors, so you can sense where each one is. Nothing else lets a blind person feel a painting's colors as warmth and cold.

name: Orca
oneLiner: You hum or beatbox a melody and say which instruments you want. It turns that into a real song you can keep editing, so you can make music without knowing any music software.
description: We're living in the golden age of creation. AI tools are unlocking creative potential like never before, but there's this massive gap: millions of enthusiast musicians and producers have incredible musical ideas stuck in their heads. You know that melody you hum in the shower? That beat you tap on your desk? Most people have no way to actually turn those into real music. We wanted to fix that. Our goal was simple: give musical enthusiasts the power to bring those fleeting melodies to life. Build something that keeps the artist in control while making the whole experience feel seamless and natural. Orca takes your voice and turns it into actual music.
whyWon: You hum or beatbox a tune and tell Orca which instruments you want, and it turns that into a real song you can keep editing. You don't need to know any music software or play any instrument. It builds a finished track from nothing but the sound you make with your voice.

name: Dance CV
oneLiner: Pick any dance video on the internet and dance along in front of your webcam. The app compares your moves to the video, gives you a score, and a voice coach tells you what to fix.
description: We were inspired by years of playing Just Dance on the Wii. It was a great way to combine fun and exercise, but we noticed two main issues. The Wii only scored you based on the position of your Wii remote, and there was no room for customization if you wanted to add your own songs and dances. To try and fix this, we decided to build our own AI dance coach that can teach you any dance in the world, and actually give you real advice that you can use to improve! Dance CV allows the user to upload an .mp4 of any solo dance they want. It will then use AI to chunk the dance into easily learnable movements so the user can spend time perfecting each move.
whyWon: You pick any dance video online and dance along in front of your webcam. Dance CV watches your whole body, scores how close you are, and a voice coach tells you exactly which moves to fix. Unlike games like Just Dance, it works with any video and actually corrects your form.

name: YES? or YES!
oneLiner: Can't make a decision? Type in your options and three live pet fish choose for you by swimming toward one. The computer then actually goes and does whatever the fish picked.
description: We wanted the most unhinged possible use of 36 hours. The premise: take an actual fish tank with three real guppies and put it in charge of a real human's real life. Over the phone, in real time, with real consequences on a real MacBook. No prompt, no UI, no off-ramp. The fish decide, your computer obeys. You call a Twilio number. A snarky AI fish picks up. You tell it about something going on in your life. A breakup, a meeting you forgot, the job you just lost. The fish picks two options for you, one slightly bad and one catastrophically bad, and shows them on a TV mounted behind a real fish tank. Three guppies swim around. Whichever side of the tank the majority of fish are on when the countdown ends, that is the option that wins.
whyWon: You give YES? or YES! a decision you're stuck on, and three real fish in a tank pick the answer by which side they swim to. The fish make the choice, and the computer carries out whatever they picked. It puts a real decision in your life in the hands of live animals.

name: ScrewYouIKEA.com
oneLiner: Upload the instruction manual for your IKEA furniture and it becomes a 3D animation. You can spin it around and watch each step happen until the furniture is fully built.
description: Furniture manual PDF → interactive 3D assembly animation. Gemini emits Scene JSON executed by a Three.js renderer.
whyWon: You upload the PDF manual for a piece of IKEA furniture and it becomes a 3D animation. You can spin it around and watch each step happen until the furniture is fully built. It turns the confusing paper instructions everyone struggles with into something you can actually watch.

name: Longshot
oneLiner: The team typed one sentence describing Minecraft, and 200 AI assistants worked together overnight, writing code side by side until they had rebuilt the game from scratch.
description: AI has already transformed the digital world by translating language to code with autonomous coding agents, but this requires human intervention. Longshot changes that by orchestrating swarms of autonomous agents. Each agent observes, interprets, and acts in real time, working in parallel while staying aligned to the project’s goal. Longshot is our architectural system curated towards spawning swarms of carefully managed long-running agents that code an end product start to finish. Most agentic coding today like Claude and OpenClaw are iterative loops where you either supervise step by step or let a single agent run in a loop.
whyWon: You type one sentence describing a game, and 200 AI coding agents work on it at the same time, all night, with no one steering them. By morning they had built a working copy of Minecraft from scratch. Other AI coding tools need you to approve each step; this one runs hundreds of them on their own.

name: Shop-A-Sketch
oneLiner: When you can't put what you want to buy into words, you just draw it. The app looks at your sketch and finds real products that look like it.
description: Traditional e-commerce search relies heavily on text. Yet, human thought is often visual, intuitive, and difficult to articulate in words. We were inspired by the gap between how people naturally imagine products and how they are forced to search for them online. Shop-A-Sketch lets users draw what they want to buy, then uses AI to interpret their sketch and search real products via Google Shopping APIs. Results show prices, ratings, and store links, and users can filter by price to refine their search. Users may then click directly into the item to purchase from the merchant. Our drawing canvas is built with React and Tailwind for a clean, responsive UI that works on any device.
whyWon: Shop-A-Sketch lets you draw the thing you want to buy instead of describing it in words. It looks at your drawing and finds real products for sale that actually look like it. It searches by what you sketch, not by words you type.

name: ScamShield
oneLiner: A device that listens along to an elderly person's phone calls. The moment a caller sounds like a scammer, it warns them with lights and a spoken alert, then texts their family.
description: Phone scams targeting older adults are one of those problems that feel invisible until it is too late. Victims are often isolated, trusting, and pressured in the moment, before a family member has any chance to step in. We built ScamShield to act like a silent guardian during those calls: something that can listen in real time, recognize scam patterns, warn the person immediately, and get their family involved before money or personal information is lost. ScamShield is a Raspberry Pi device that listens to calls on speakerphone, detects scam patterns using AI, and instantly alerts both the person on the call and their family. There is no app to install and no major behavior change required.
whyWon: ScamShield is a small device that listens along to an older person's phone calls. The moment a caller sounds like a scammer, it warns them out loud and with lights, and texts their family right away. It steps in during the call itself, before any money or information is lost.

name: Snowy Day
oneLiner: After a big snowstorm, this app replans the routes snow plows take through the city, so streets get cleared faster and no neighborhood gets forgotten.
description: Our streets turned into chaos, traffic slowed to a crawl, and snowplows somehow still missed half the roads. We started wondering: why are snowplow routes still basically guesswork? That curiosity turned into Snowy Day. A fun experiment to see how much better snowplowing could be with a little AI help. Snowy Day lets you pick a city and watch snowplows clean it up the smart way. Each snowplow acts like its own little brain, figuring out where to go next so that every road gets cleared with as little overlap and wasted time as possible. You can watch the plows move in real time as they tackle streets, highways, and intersections together.
whyWon: Snowy Day plans the routes snowplows take through a city so every street gets cleared and none get skipped. Each plow works out where to go to avoid overlap and wasted time, and you watch them do it in real time. Most cities still plan plow routes by guesswork; this replaces that.

name: Wordhawk
oneLiner: A small robot that plays the iPhone game Word Hunt by itself. A camera reads the letter board, the software finds the words, and a robotic finger swipes them on screen faster than any human.
description: A robot that plays Word Hunt on its own-powered by a jury-rigged 3D printer, computer vision, and distributed intelligence. We combined mechanical automation, edge AI, and language models to create a system that not only finds the best words on the board but also teaches you what they mean. Our project automates the process of solving Word Hunt, a gamepigeon (iMessages) word game, using a fully integrated hardware + software pipeline: Capture. A photo of the Word Hunt board is taken. Recognize. An Edge AI accelerator runs OCR locally to extract letters from the board in real time. Detect. Using a DFS with a trie, the system searches the board for all valid words.
award: Best Use of MemryX Edge AI Accelerator (122 teams)
whyWon: Wordhawk is a robot that plays the phone game Word Hunt by itself. A camera reads the letters on the screen, the software finds the words, and a robotic finger swipes them out faster than a person can. It doesn't just solve the puzzle, it physically plays the game on a real phone.

name: diffuji
oneLiner: An instant camera that prints art instead of photos. You snap a picture, the camera repaints it in a dreamy art style, and it prints the result on receipt paper while you wait.
description: The world needs a bit more silliness. Instant cams are fun, and there’s something special about memorializing a moment through physical media. But what if these moments were … part dream, part hallucination of reality? Introducing Diffuji - a diffusion-powered instant camera that turns half-real, half-dreamed moments into physical prints. You can take a picture and choose from a range of filters to apply to your image. However, these filters aren’t your typical color grades, but can completely reimagine the context the photo was taken in - whether turning back time to the 19th century, having the best six-pack you’ve never had, or simply becoming a duck.
whyWon: diffuji is an instant camera that prints art instead of photos. You take a picture and it repaints it in a dreamy style, then prints the result on receipt paper while you wait. You walk away holding a physical print, not just an image on a screen.

name: freak-cha
oneLiner: A test that proves you're a real human and not an AI. Instead of clicking pictures of traffic lights, you answer by sticking out your tongue in different directions, which deepfakes can't fake.
description: In the age of SORA 2 and Google Veo 3, deepfakes have blurred the line between human and AI-generated faces. Our inspiration for Freak-cha came from iOS 26’s accessibility tongue-scrolling feature. An innovative way to interact with technology using subtle gestures. We wanted to take that idea further by designing a verification system that AI simply couldn’t replicate. Freak-cha is a tongue-based biometric authentication system that challenges users to prove their humanity through facial expressions and tongue gestures, turning security into a playful, interactive experience. We built Freak-cha as a CAPTCHA process with multiple stages.
whyWon: freak-cha is a test that proves you're a real person and not an AI. Instead of clicking pictures, you stick your tongue out in different directions on camera. It works because deepfakes still can't fake tongue movements, so it checks for the one thing AI can't do yet.

name: HeartStart
oneLiner: A robot that watches for someone's heart stopping. When it happens, it drives itself across the room to the person and starts doing CPR while the ambulance is on the way.
description: Every year, more than 350,000 people in the US experience cardiac arrest outside of a hospital setting. The difference between life and death often comes down to those critical minutes before emergency services arrive-minutes where CPR must be performed continuously, consistently, and correctly. But bystanders freeze, panic, or simply don't know how to administer CPR properly. First responders face impossible response times. And even trained individuals grow fatigued, their compressions becoming shallow and ineffective after just two minutes. In fact, according to the American Heart Association, the survival rate drops by 7-10% for every minute without CPR. After just 10 minutes, survival is nearly zero.
whyWon: HeartStart is a robot that watches for someone's heart stopping. When it happens, it drives itself across the room to the person and starts doing chest compressions on its own. It keeps CPR going in the minutes before an ambulance arrives, when most bystanders freeze or don't know how.

name: LifeSaver
oneLiner: Lets medical students practice surgery like a video game. The webcam tracks their hands while a voice guides them through each step, and they get a score at the end, with no real patient needed.
description: A medical student spends 4 years in school, then 3-7 years in residency, and for most of that time they're watching not doing. The first time many residents actually hold a laparoscopic instrument and cut tissue, there's a real patient on the table. That's terrifying also Surgical simulation exists, but it costs $100,000+ per machine. Most medical schools have one or two simulators shared across hundreds of students, and you get maybe 30 minutes a semester on them. The rest of the time you're watching YouTube videos of surgeries and hoping you remember the steps when it matters. And it's not just about access it's about feedback.
whyWon: LifeSaver lets medical students practice surgery using just a webcam. It tracks their hands, a voice walks them through each step, and they get a score at the end, with no real patient involved. The machines that normally do this cost over $100,000; this does it on a laptop.

name: kinemo
oneLiner: A party game console that needs no controllers at all. The webcam watches your body, so you jump, duck, and wave your arms to play the games.
description: We started off making a boxing motion tracker with the intention of creating real-time AI feedback. It slowly evolved into feeling more like a game, so we decided to work towards that instead. Eventually, we realized we could turn this into something bigger. Then the idea for a whole console-like experience with multiple games was born. Our project is a motion-controlled gaming platform that only needs a webcam. Two players can compete in three different active games. The main menu is controlled with hand gestures, letting players choose between Boxing, Flying Fruits, or Reaction Time. In Boxing, players can punch, kick, or weave. Each player starts with 12 hearts. A punch takes away 1 heart, and a kick takes away 3.
whyWon: kinemo is a party game console you play with your body and a webcam, no controllers at all. You jump, duck, and swing your arms to play the games. It gives you a whole console's worth of games using only the camera you already have.

name: Artificial Sandwich Intelligence
oneLiner: A robot that learned to make sandwiches just by watching people make them for four hours. It was built from robot arms that cost only $200.
description: Imitation-learning sandwich robot trained from 4 hours of teleop on $200 arms. Robot learning is now weekend-scale.
whyWon: This robot learned to make sandwiches just by watching people make them for four hours. It runs on robot arms that cost $200 total, not lab equipment. It shows you can teach a robot a real hands-on task in a weekend for almost no money.

name: Get Clipped!
oneLiner: An app that keeps you honest while studying. If you quit your study session early, it takes a photo of you and sends it to your friends so they know you gave up.
description: We all have that one friend who says they’re "locked in" on studying, but five minutes later, they’re doomscrolling on TikTok. Nowadays, individual willpower isn't enough to stop the urge to check your phone. We wanted to solve this in a unique and entertaining way. We realized that for some people, the best motivator isn't a productivity graph, but rather is the fear of getting exposed in front of your closest friends. Get Clipped turns a study session into a high-stakes standoff where if one person folds, everyone knows immediately and instantly get a clipped photo of the person who folded. Users log in (via Firebase Auth) and add their friends to a study group.
whyWon: Get Clipped! keeps you focused while studying with your friends. If you quit your study session early, it takes a photo of you and sends it to the group so everyone sees you gave up. Instead of charts and streaks, it uses the fear of getting caught by your friends.

name: Wing
oneLiner: A safety app for people living with an abusive partner. It looks and works exactly like a normal weather app, so if the abuser checks the phone, nothing seems unusual.
description: We were motivated by the number of women who experience intimate partner violence and the aid workers who seek to provide help in secret, providing victims with ways to report their situation without alerting the abuser or raising suspicion. However, we hope that this project can provide help to anyone in an unsafe environment. We built this project to provide aid to individuals in restrictive and unhealthy relationships, providing a private and hidden outlet to chat, find help and respond in emergency situations. The app showcases a fake interface: it would appear as a Weather App to outsiders, but has hidden, protected settings that let the user seek help, find available resources and chat.
whyWon: Wing is a safety app for people living with an abuser, hidden inside a working weather app. To anyone who looks, it's just a normal weather app, but the person can quietly reach help and emergency contacts behind it. The disguise means it's safe to keep on the phone even if the abuser checks it.

name: Ted.AI
oneLiner: A teddy bear that can tell how you're feeling when you hug it, using your heartbeat and how tightly you squeeze. It talks back gently, like a friend who always knows when something's wrong.
description: Every day, millions of children grow up battling unseen struggles. Anxiety, ADHD, loneliness, and emotional overwhelm. In a world moving too fast to listen. We wanted to create something that not only hears them but feels with them. A companion that bridges empathy and intelligence to help them regulate emotions and feel understood. Ted.AI is an emotionally intelligent teddy bear powered by multimodal AI. It senses hugs, movement, and heart rate using embedded sensors and responds with empathy through a conversational LLM. It guides children through breathing exercises, focus routines, and calming interactions while analyzing tone, language, and gestures to adapt its responses in real time.
whyWon: Ted.AI is a teddy bear that can tell how a child is feeling when they hug it. It senses how tight the squeeze is and how fast their heart is beating, then talks back gently. It reads emotions from the hug itself, so the child never has to put how they feel into words.

name: Maestro
oneLiner: Point a camera at a broom and play it like a real guitar. It tracks your hands and turns air-strumming into actual notes and chords, and whatever you play can grow into a fully produced song.
description: We’re an international team, and music is one of the few languages everyone on our team shares. No matter where we’re from, we’ve all used everyday objects, desks, bottles, and brooms to make rhythm or pretend instruments. We wanted to build something that lets anyone express themselves musically and develop their musical skills without needing expensive gear or formal training. Maestro turns a broom into a playable guitar and music studio. Using a camera, hand tracking, and sound mapping, we detect a broom and map your hand movements to musical notes so you can instantly play it like a real instrument. An iPhone companion detects pressed “strings,” letting you form chords and control pitch while strumming in the air.

team.name: Gaston Nils Marie de Baudry d'Asson

whyWon: You point a camera at any object, like a broom, and play it like a guitar. Maestro tracks your hands and turns your air-strumming into real notes and chords, which can grow into a full song. It turns everyday objects into instruments, so you don't need to own one.

name: ViniClip
oneLiner: Press record and just talk. If you stumble, pause, or restart a sentence, the app cuts those parts out while you're still recording, so the moment you stop you have a clean, finished video ready to post.
description: Ever fumble through a video recording, pause awkwardly, or restart mid-sentence? Typical video editing means recording everything, then spending hours re-recording or cutting out mistakes - but what if your video could edit itself while you recorded? ViniClip is an opinionated, automatic video editor that cuts out pauses and errors on the fly. Record yourself directly in your browser, and ViniClip removes awkward pauses, repeated takes, or any mistake you wish to get rid of instantly. The final product is a video that's ready to post immediately, you can even command the video to be upload to Instagram reels instantly. This project enables real-time video editing during active recording sessions.
whyWon: ViniClip edits your video while you're still recording it. If you stumble, pause, or restart a sentence, it cuts those parts out on the spot, so the moment you stop, the video is finished. Normal editing happens after you record; this happens as you talk.

name: Plant Hopper
oneLiner: A robotic water-gun turret for your houseplants. It looks around the room, figures out which plants are thirsty, then aims and squirts each one with exactly the water it needs.
description: Traditional watering methods like sprinklers waste up to 50% of the water they use. They treat every plant the same, ignoring that some need more or less water, or operate on different schedules. We wanted a setup that’s smarter, healthier for plants, and massively more efficient. Plant Hopper solves this by individually targeting plants using computer vision, optimized schedules, and soil moisture readings. The system scales across multiple plants using a database-driven model instead of fixed hardware zones.
whyWon: Plant Hopper is a robotic water-gun turret for your houseplants. It looks around the room, works out which plants are thirsty, then aims and squirts each one the exact amount it needs. It waters each plant individually instead of soaking them all the same.

name: ChessMate
oneLiner: A real chessboard where the pieces slide across the board by themselves, moved by a magnet underneath. You play against family on the other side of the world, and their moves appear on your board like magic.
description: Gantry + electromagnet chessboard for playing family abroad. Physical and digital state stay in sync.
whyWon: ChessMate is a real chessboard where the pieces slide around by themselves. When you play someone far away, a magnet under the board moves their pieces so both boards always match. You play long-distance chess on a real board instead of on a screen.

name: BrailleBot
oneLiner: Braille printers normally cost about $1,500, so this team built one for under $15 from a cheap 3D printer. Feed it any document and it punches the whole thing out in braille, even describing the pictures in plain words.
description: During the start of Treehacks, we chatted with a fellow builder who described corresponding with her visually-impaired pen pal: She types her message on her computer, and then manually punches the braille, letter by letter, on a sheet of paper to send over. Why does she do this? Braille printers are outrageously expensive due to the high degree of precision required and the niche market. Due to the high cost, many who seek to print braille documents instead manually punch each letter with a specialized hole puncher. This struggle extends far beyond personal correspondence-it highlights a critical gap in education for the visually impaired.
whyWon: BrailleBot is a braille printer built from a cheap 3D printer for under $15. You feed it any document and it punches the whole thing out in braille, and it even describes the pictures in words. Real braille printers cost around $1,500; this does the same job for about a hundredth of the price.

name: Fable
oneLiner: You wear a headband that reads your brainwaves while a story unfolds on screen. The plot, pictures, and sound change depending on how focused or relaxed you actually are, so no two people ever experience the same story.
description: Fable began with a simple question: What if a story could adapt to your state of mind? As our team explored EEG technology, we became excited about the idea of measuring brain waves and uncovering hidden patterns of thought. What if a narrative could respond to your level of focus and relaxation-your shifting mental state? With EEG headsets reading brain activity, Fable dynamically shapes its plot, characters, sound effects, and visuals, crafting a living, breathing story that unfolds uniquely for you. This isn’t just storytelling-it’s an experience powered by your mind. Fable reads your brainwaves to craft a living story that responds to your every thought.
whyWon: Fable reads your brainwaves with a headband and changes the story as you go. The plot, pictures, and music shift depending on how focused or relaxed you are. Nothing else makes a story react to your brain in real time.

name: Stu3dio
oneLiner: A tiny 3D movie studio you walk around in, staffed by virtual workers. You chat with the director about the story, the writer about dialogue, and the artist about your cast, and out comes a finished short film.
description: Make an AI movie by talking to others in the 3D studio. First, chat with an Director to brainstorm ideas, build characters, and outline scenes. Secondly, collaborate with an Scriptwriter to flesh out dialogue, actions, and narrative details. Next, meet the Character Artist, where you can tweak style, clothes (ANYTHING YOU WANT) by simply scribbling on the character’s image for instant AI edits. Lastly, view a timeline of all the scenes and their frames, you could edit the scene images directly using scribbling tools and getting instant responses from our image gen AI. Finally ... generate the video ...
whyWon: Stu3dio is a little 3D movie studio you walk around in. You talk to a director about the story, a writer about the lines, and an artist about your characters, and it makes the finished short film. Instead of typing into a chat box, you make a movie by walking around and talking to the staff.

name: SignHero
oneLiner: A rhythm game that teaches you sign language. Letters scroll down the screen like falling notes, and you score points by making each hand sign in time with the music while your webcam checks you got it right.
description: Over 48 million Americans are deaf or hard of hearing, yet ASL literacy remains low among the hearing population. We wanted to make learning ASL fingerspelling fun, accessible, and engaging -the same way Guitar Hero made learning rhythm patterns addictive. The breakthrough idea: What if we could gamify sign language learning with real-time AI detection and rhythm-based gameplay? SignHero is a rhythm game that teaches ASL fingerspelling through webcam-powered gameplay: The AI watches your webcam, detects which ASL letter you're signing in real-time (~30-50ms latency), and scores your accuracy with combo multipliers, streak celebrations, and visual effects. For this we build a custom model based on the mobileneyb2 cnn as our backbone.
whyWon: SignHero teaches sign language as a rhythm game. Letters fall down the screen like notes and you score by making the right hand sign in time with the music, while your webcam checks you got it right. It turns practicing sign language into a game you actually want to keep playing.

name: S-KBD67
oneLiner: They hacked a Nerf gun into a video game controller. Pulling the trigger shoots in the game, pumping it reloads, and pointing it aims, so you play shooter games with a real toy gun in your hands.
description: "What if you could play an FPS game IRL" was the question that inspired us. Our goal was to enhance the mechanics of an FPS (first person shooter) game into real life using a Nerf blaster. The gameplay turns into something significantly more realistic and immersive. The system lets players control an FPS game using only a Nerf gun (and a bunch of other cool things). Inputs and motions are translated into the game: aiming, turning, pressing our feature buttons and joystick to move, reload and switch weapons. Instead of using a mouse, you can just shoot a (toy) gun, creating a smooth and satisfying connection between the physical and digital experience playing, We divided into hardware and backend teams.
whyWon: This team turned a Nerf gun into a controller for shooter video games. Pulling the trigger shoots in the game, pumping it reloads, and pointing it aims. You play with a real toy gun in your hands instead of a mouse and keyboard.

name: Rehabify
oneLiner: Doing physical therapy at home, you never know if you're doing the exercises right. This app watches through your webcam and corrects you out loud the moment your form slips, like having a therapist in the room.
description: After spending $8K on physio bills, waiting 3 weeks for my first appointment, and only getting seen once every 2 weeks for a herniated disc, I realized the broken part of physical therapy isn't the clinic, it's what happens at home. Only 35% of patients complete their prescribed exercises because they're unsure about their form and have zero supervision between visits. Physiotherapists (PTs) have no visibility into whether patients are doing exercises correctly, incorrectly, or at all. We built Rehabify with a focus on security and privacy to make quality PT accessible to everyone and enhance patient outcomes. Rehabify is an AI-powered physical therapy coach that provides real-time form correction using just a webcam.
whyWon: Rehabify watches you do your physical therapy exercises at home through your webcam. The moment your form slips, it tells you out loud how to fix it, like a therapist in the room. It gives you feedback during the weeks between appointments, when normally no one is checking.

name: ReelJobs
oneLiner: Instead of reading walls of text on job boards, you swipe through a feed of short videos where every job listing has been turned into a 20-second clip. When you see one you like, one tap fills out the application for you.
description: Job hunting sucks. You scroll through endless walls of text, copy-paste the same resume into a dozen forms, and still hear nothing back. Meanwhile, we spend hours on TikTok and Instagram Reels consuming content effortlessly. We asked ourselves: what if finding your next job felt as easy as scrolling through Reels? Gen Z and millennials already consume information through short-form video. Why should job discovery be stuck in the 2010s? ReelJobs brings job listings into the format we actually enjoy consuming. ReelJobs transforms job listings into engaging short-form vertical videos. Instead of reading through job descriptions, you swipe through 15-30 second video summaries that capture the essence of each role.
whyWon: ReelJobs turns job listings into short videos you swipe through like TikTok. Each one is a 20-second clip, and when you see a job you like, one tap fills out the application. It replaces scrolling walls of text and filling out forms with swiping a video feed.

name: The Unspillable
oneLiner: A spoon that keeps its bowl perfectly level no matter how much your hand shakes, so people with tremors can eat without spilling. The real product costs over $300; this team built one in a weekend with a tiny computer chip and two small motors.
description: When researching solutions to Parkinson's and hand tremors, we realized that spoons with actual self-stabilizing technology were very expensive, often easily above $300. Thus, we decided to try to make one with easily accessible microcontrollers, motors and sensors to make an affordable version. The Unspillable is a smart spoon that uses a 2-axis sensor-motor system to counteract tremors and tilts in real time. The spoon bowl always remains perfectly level no matter the angle of the handle or hand. We wired together an Arduino Nano, a MPU6050 sensor, two servo motors, a button and a battery to be the core self-stabilizing system. Then we used CAD to create a case for it all and 3D printed it so we could use our system as the handle.
whyWon: The Unspillable is a spoon that stays level no matter how badly your hand shakes, so people with tremors can eat without spilling. Two small motors counteract the shaking in real time. The version sold in stores costs over $300; this team built a working one in a weekend for a few dollars.

name: Project Horizon
oneLiner: A soccer game where you can watch the match through the eyes of any player on the field. Instead of looking down from above, you become the striker or the keeper and experience the game as them.
description: Have you ever watched Messi and wondered what the game looks like from his point of view? Project Horizon explores identity through perspective. In sports, a player’s identity is shaped not only by their actions, but by how they perceive the game: positioning, timing, awareness, and decision-making. Reconstructing a first-person POV from third-person footage using novel machine learning techniques, we let viewers momentarily inhabit another identity. Project Horizon is an AI system that converts third-person videos into realistic first-person POV footage. Users can select any player in a soccer video and experience the game exactly as that player would see it.
whyWon: Project Horizon lets you watch a soccer match through the eyes of any player on the field. Instead of the usual view from above, you see the game the way the striker or the keeper sees it, built from normal TV footage. It puts you inside a player's point of view from video that was never filmed that way.

name: Coach Bob!
oneLiner: A punching bag with a personality. Through AR glasses, glowing targets light up on the bag showing where to punch, it measures how hard you hit, and Bob trash-talks you to keep you moving.
description: One of our teammates had to sit out of Taekwondo Nationals due to a strain injury. The cause? A rushed 5-minute warm-up that just wasn’t enough. It was a harsh reminder: skimping on warm-ups (Even if it's boring) is a common mistake, but it leads to real consequences...injury, lost training time, and missed opportunities. It's also a great way for people to get active and stay healthy! We set out to turn that mundane yet critical step into something athletes actually look forward to: an interactive AR warm-up game. By combining guided drills, live feedback, and playful challenges, our system makes proper warm-ups engaging, adaptive, and fun-so athletes are ready to perform and stay safe.
whyWon: Coach Bob! is a punching bag you train with through AR glasses. Glowing targets light up on the bag to show where to hit, it measures how hard you punch, and Bob talks trash to keep you going. It turns a boring warm-up into a game so people actually do it.

name: WaterYouDoin
oneLiner: Every question you ask an AI uses real water to cool the computers that answer it. This browser add-on catches questions a normal search could answer, nudges you to skip them, and shows the water you saved as little ice cubes.
description: AI tools are incredibly powerful, but we noticed developers often use them for things that don’t actually need AI, like definitions, quick lookups, or repetitive prompts. We wanted to encourage more intentional AI use without blocking or judging developers. Encouraging a more productive use of AI and avoid rabbit holes of intellectual doom. WaterYourDoin is a browser extension that locally analyzes prompts before they’re sent to an AI assistant. All decisions happen locally, and the extension visualizes avoided AI usage as water savings using a fun ice-cube mascot. We built a Chrome extension using TypeScript and Vite. Prompt classification runs entirely in the browser using lightweight NLP heuristics with an optional ML fallback.
whyWon: Every question you ask an AI uses real water to cool the computers behind it. WaterYouDoin is a browser add-on that catches questions a normal search could answer and shows how much water you save by skipping them, stacked up as ice cubes. It makes a hidden cost most people don't know about something you can see.

name: Vril
oneLiner: Type a description of any object and it becomes a real 3D model you can tweak and send straight to a 3D printer, no design software needed.
description: People have ideas for physical products but lack the 3D modeling skills or time to design them. Even simple items like mugs or boxes require specialized tools, long iteration cycles, and expensive designers. We wanted to make product design something anyone could do. Like how Lovable makes coding accessible for non-technical people. Vril lets users create and edit full 3D product models and packaging by describing what they want in plain English or uploading a reference. It also includes an editor where users can iterate the shape, materials, and packaging with AI. Generates editable 3D models from text or reference images. Includes a prompt-driven editor similar to Cursor for adjusting geometry and materials.
whyWon: With Vril you describe an object in plain words and it gives you a real 3D model. You can tweak it and send it straight to a 3D printer, with no design software needed. It lets people with no modeling skills design something they can actually print.

name: Physical Digital Darts (Wii Darts)
oneLiner: They built a real dart with a motion sensor inside, so you swing your arm like you're actually throwing and the dart flies across a dartboard on screen. It's like building your own Wii controller from scratch.
description: As a team who values accessible and immersive gaming experiences, we wanted to create a hack that embodied both of these ideas across software, hardware, and design. Taking inspiration from the Nintendo Wii and Switch consoles, we choose to create a Physical Digital Dart which allows the user to perform throwing motions as they would in real life, captures that motion with an accelerometer, and simulates the result on a digital dartboard.
whyWon: This team built a real dart with a motion sensor inside it. You wind up and throw like you actually would, and the dart flies across a dartboard on the screen. They built their own Wii-style motion controller from scratch instead of buying one.

name: BlinkAI
oneLiner: For people who can't move or speak, this app turns blinking into a voice. You blink short and long like Morse code, and it speaks full sentences out loud for you.
description: For millions of people living with Parkinson’s, ALS, and other mobility challenges, something as simple as sending a message can feel overwhelming. Most digital assistants rely on voice or typing, but what if those barriers didn’t exist at all? That’s why we created Blink AI-not just another assistant, but an intelligent system powered by a groundbreaking translation algorithm that converts blinks into text, opening a world of possibilities for those who need it most. Because communication should never be limited by physical ability. Everyone deserves a way to express their thoughts, ask questions, and stay connected-and now, they can do it with just a blink.
whyWon: BlinkAI lets people who can't move or speak talk by blinking. You blink short and long, like Morse code, and a webcam reads it and speaks full sentences out loud. It gives someone a voice using only their eyes, with no special equipment.

name: Project Lend
oneLiner: A food bank with no people in it. Donors text in what they have, a robot arm sorts the food into boxes, and AI helpers arrange the pickups. Over one weekend it delivered about 100 pounds of food to real shelters.
description: We've spent years volunteering at food banks and shelters, experiencing firsthand the challenges of both front-office admin and back-breaking logistics. When Anthropic released Project Vend showing Claude could run a business autonomously, we wondered- what if we applied that same capability to helping people give instead of take? With agents now able to reason, communicate, and operate in the physical world, Project Lend was born at the intersection of technical possibility and human need. Project Lend is an autonomous food bank run entirely by AI.
whyWon: Project Lend is a food bank that runs with almost no people. Donors text what they have, a robot arm sorts the food into boxes, and AI arranges the pickups and deliveries. Over one weekend it actually delivered about 100 pounds of food to real shelters.

name: GhostDieDie
oneLiner: A 3D fighting game where you and a friend battle by physically moving in front of your webcams. You see each other's real faces on the fighters while you dodge and swing.
description: Ghostdiedie turns the laptop webcam everyone already has into a real-time controller for competitive multiplayer fighting. The name reflects exactly what the game feels like. Ghost means your opponent is not physically standing in front of you. You are looking at a screen and throwing punches and kicks into empty space. Die die comes from the Korean slang 다이다이 , which refers to a one-on-one fight, face to face. Put together, Ghostdiedie means fighting someone who is not present in your real space while still facing them directly in a one-on-one match. That is exactly the feeling the game creates.
whyWon: GhostDieDie is a fighting game you play by physically moving in front of your webcam. You and a friend throw real punches and dodge, and you see each other's actual faces on the fighters. Your whole body is the controller, using just the webcam you already have.

name: Lume
oneLiner: A patient voice helper for grandparents using a computer. Ask it how to unmute on Zoom and it draws a glowing highlight right on the button to click, like a tech-savvy grandkid who is always around.
description: We’ve all been there: helping a parent or grandparent unmute on Zoom, find the “Share Screen” button, or open an email attachment. Small tech tasks that take seconds for us can feel impossible for older adults, creating frustration and dependence. We wanted to change that. Lume was born from the idea of a patient, tech-savvy helper--like a grandkid who’s always available--that can listen, see, and guide seniors through technology with confidence. Lume is a voice-activated, on-screen tutor that shows seniors exactly where to click. It listens to natural questions (“How do I join a Zoom meeting?”), uses AI to understand what’s on their screen, and then visually highlights the correct buttons step by step--while speaking instructions aloud.
whyWon: Lume is a helper for older people using a computer. You ask it something like how to unmute on Zoom, and it draws a glowing circle right on the button you need to click. It shows you the exact button on your real screen instead of just describing the steps.

name: Replay
oneLiner: Record a real room with a phone and two small sensors, then replay the moment in 3D. You can pause, rewind, and fly the camera anywhere inside the memory, like a video game replay of real life.
description: Everyone who's played Minecraft knows Replay Mod. You record a session, then scrub through time and fly a free camera around the 3D world to relive it from any angle. We asked: what if we could do that with real life? Record a physical space and the people moving through it, then replay the moment in an interactive 3D viewer where you can pause, rewind, and orbit freely. No volumetric video studio, no professional mocap suit. Just an iPhone, two ESP32s, and a laptop. Replay captures a real-world 3D environment and human body motion in real time, then lets you replay it as an interactive 3D scene.
whyWon: Replay records a real room and the people in it using a phone and two small sensors. Afterward you can replay the moment in 3D, pausing, rewinding, and moving the camera anywhere you want. It lets you fly through a real memory like a video game replay.

name: EcoTag
oneLiner: Portable laser tag where you reload by picking up real litter and scanning it into the game. Cleaning the park stops being a chore because every piece of trash powers your next round.
description: 🏆 StormHacks Winner. Selected as one of the top projects among 778 participants at Western Canada’s largest hackathon. 🌍 Winner of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDG) Challenge. Recognized for creating an innovative and scalable solution that advances sustainability and positive social impact. EcoTag. Portable laser tag that turns “ugh, cleanup” into “one more round,” helping the planet one scan at a time. EcoTag is the world’s first portable, phone-powered laser tag fused with real-world environmental action. Attach a simple QR tag, scan to score hits and climb the leaderboard, then flip the script: scan real litter, classify it, get CO₂-saving estimates, and drop it in the right bin for bonus points.
whyWon: EcoTag is portable laser tag where you reload by picking up real litter and scanning it. Every piece of trash you collect gives you more ammo for the next round. It turns cleaning up a park into the game itself.

name: Dose
oneLiner: A pill bottle that weighs itself to know whether grandma took her medicine today. If a dose is missed, the family gets a message before it becomes a problem.
description: Medication non-adherence is a silent but massive issue. Studies show that 30-50% of patients with chronic conditions fail to follow their prescribed regimens, and nearly half of study participants intentionally skip doses. The impact is staggering: non-adherence costs the U.S. healthcare system an estimated $100-$300 billion annually in avoidable expenses. Dose was born from the need for a smarter, scalable solution to address this challenge. Dose is a smart medication adherence system that pairs a sensor-enabled pill bottle with an interactive web dashboard. The pill bottle uses a load cell sensor to detect pill movements in real time, producing an accurate, tamper-resistant record of medication usage.
whyWon: Dose is a pill bottle that weighs itself to tell whether someone took their medicine. If the bottle didn't get lighter that day, a dose was missed and the family gets a text. It figures out whether grandma took her pills from weight alone, with nothing for her to do.

name: WhaleBeing
oneLiner: Cargo ships accidentally hit and kill blue whales on busy routes. This app redraws shipping routes around the places whales feed and travel, so ships lose barely any time and whales keep their lives.
description: Up to 20,000 whales per year are killed by ships globally, posing a significant threat to already vulnerable whale populations. The issue of ship strikes is not only an tragedy for marine ecosystems but also an avoidable one: in many cases, reducing ship speeds and rerouting to avoid high-risk areas comes at little to no cost to ship operators. To solve this problem, we built WhaleBeing. WhaleBeing uses a dynamic prediction model to display how ship routes interact with blue whale habitats using a web app.
whyWon: WhaleBeing redraws cargo ship routes so they avoid the places whales feed and travel. Ships lose barely any time, and far fewer whales get hit and killed. It shows a deadly problem could be fixed at almost no cost to the ships.

name: ARchemy
oneLiner: A crafting game pulled into the real world. Through AR glasses you grab floating objects with your bare hands and smash them together to invent new ones, so a duck plus a car becomes a quackmobile.
description: Our team really enjoyed playing games like Little Alchemy, and Infinite Craft, where we get to take basic building blocks and challenge the limits of our imagination. But we wanted to take this to the next level. What if instead of clicking pixels on a screen with a mouse, we move these building blocks into the real world? What if we could spawn anything from a toaster, to a dragon, to a transformer right before our eyes, all with the use of our fingertips? Well, you can achieve all of this through ARchemy. ARchemy is an augmented reality crafting game that lets players combine elemental building blocks to create new ones in real time.
whyWon: ARchemy is the game Infinite Craft brought into the real world. Through AR glasses you grab floating objects with your bare hands and smash them together to invent new ones, so a duck and a car make a quackmobile. You play it with your actual hands in the air instead of clicking on a screen.

name: ResQme
oneLiner: When a disaster knocks out cell towers and wifi, these cheap pocket radios keep working. They pass your texts and location from one device to the next until they reach rescuers.
description: In disaster zones, communication often fails first. We wanted to create a system that doesn’t rely on cell towers or the internet, something people and rescue teams could still use to coordinate when everything else goes down. ResQMe is a mesh network built on ESP32 devices. Each node can send and relay messages through others, forming a local communication web that works even without infrastructure. The master node can host a Wi-Fi access point with a simple webpage for users to send messages or mark their status. We used ESP32 boards running a PainlessMesh network for communication. The master node manages message distribution and can log activity over serial for cloud mirroring. Each device can also run in AP mode for local access.
whyWon: ResQme is a set of cheap pocket radios that keep working when cell towers and wifi go down. Each one passes your text and location to the next device until it reaches rescuers. It keeps people connected in a disaster exactly when every normal network has failed.
